4.8. THE CONGRUENCE DOMAIN

Congruence domain

 ̆
element, meaning “no information” and a widening can default to € if no meaningful

4.8

The Congruence Domain

The last non-relational abstract domain we present is a congruence domain, specific to
integers, introduced by Granger [1989]. We thus assume I = Z, and infer value abstractions
of the form X œ aZ + b, meaning that X equals some multiple of a plus b. This domain is
particularly useful to track pointer offsets in arrays and data-structures, prove alignment
properties, or infer array access patterns. We set as abstract values:
 ̆ def

3

B = { (aZ + b) | a œ N, b œ Z } fi { ‹ }
 ̆

(4.15)

===== slide 4 =====
Congruence domain
abstract elements of the form aZ + b
(with a ∈ N, b ∈ Z)

γ( ⊥ ) = ∅
γ(aZ+b) = {ak+b ∣ k ∈ Z}multiples of a
with offset b
e.g.

γ(2Z+0) = set of even numbers
γ(2Z+1) = set of odd numbers
γ(0Z+42) = {42}
4

===== slide 5 =====
Congruence: concretization
γ is not injective
γ(2Z + 1) = γ(2Z + 3) = γ(2Z − 1)
γ(aZ + b) = γ(aZ + (b + ak)) for any k ∈ Z
sometimes is assumed 0 ≤ b < a (when a ≠ 0)
then γ becomes injective
5

===== slide 6 =====
Congruence domain
• the greatest element is 1Z + 0
• precise on singletons:
singleton {c} is represented as 0Z + c
• aZ + b ⊑ a′Z + b′ iff γ(aZ + b) ⊆ γ(a′Z + b′)

• the bottom element is explictly added

#

Although the domain has an infinite height, there is no infinite strictly increasing chain!
Why?
• moving up in the lattice = losing modular constraints
• losing constraints = the modulus a decreases
• but it cannot decrease indefinitely

6Z ⊏ 3Z ⊏ 1Z

Example 4.11 (Congruence analysis). Consider the program:

X is even

X Ω 0; Y Ω 2;
X ∈ (2Z + 0)
while X < 40 do inferred loop invariant:
Y
∈

+
2)
X Ω X + 2;
if X < 5 then Y Ω Y + 18 endif ;
gcd(18,30)

done
or decremented by 30 at any iteration

Note that, except when an abstract value represents a constant, any test of the form X < 40
or X > 8 is abstracted as the identity. Our static analysis will infer, as loop invariant,
X œ 2Z + 0, i.e., X is even, and Y œ 6Z + 2, where 6 is the gcd of 18 and 30 as, at any
loop iteration, Y may be incremented by 18 or decremented by 30.

===== slide 14 =====
Convex Polyhedra domain
sets of numerical constraints of the form

c1x + c2y ≤ c

(at most two variables per constraint, with coefficients)
is a relational domain, can be used
when intervals are not expressive enough

14

x, y ∈ [0,5]
1x + 0y ≤ 5
−1x + 0y ≤ 0
0x + 1y ≤ 5
0x + −1y ≤ 0

x=y
1x + −1y ≤ 0
−1x + 1y ≤ 0

===== slide 17 =====
No best approximation
Computing the best approximation is not always possible

In practice we can use abstractions
as precise as possible, but maybe not the best
(still best abstractions can exist for some sets of states)
17

===== slide 18 =====
Why is the Polyhedra Domain Expensive?
Combinatorial explosion
• number of constraints can be exponential
• H-representation V-representation is costly
Expensive operations
• Join (convex hull) → may generate many constraints
• Projection → exponential blow-up (Fourier–Motzkin)
Costly normalization
• removing redundant constraints
• requires linear programming
Inclusion checks
• need to solve LP problems

===== slide 19 =====
Octagons domain
sets of numerical constraints of the form

±x ± y ≤ c

(at most two variables per constraint, with unit coefficients)
is a relational domain, can be used
when intervals are not expressive enough
and polyhedra are more costly
(allow trading precision for efficiency)
19

1x + −1y ≤ 0
−1x + 1y ≤ 0

−1x + 1y ≤ 1

===== slide 28 =====
Conjunctive properties
program verification often requires the use
of logical predicates that correspond to the
conjunction of several basic predicates
concrete domain = powerset of integers
abstract domain = product domain:
intervals abstraction × congruence abstraction
28

===== slide 29 =====
Cartesian product

Given the (complete) lattices ( Li , ⊑i , ⊔i , ⊓i , ⊥i , ⊤i ) for i = 1,2
We derive their cartesian product lattice ( L, ⊑, ⊔, ⊓, ⊥, ⊤ ) by taking:

• L ≜ L1 × L2 ;
• (a1, a2) ⊑ (b1, b2) iff a1 ⊑1 b1 and a2 ⊑2 b2 ;
• (a1, a2) ⊔ (b1, b2) ≜ ( a1 ⊔1 b1 , a2 ⊔2 b2 ) ;
• (a1, a2) ⊓ (b1, b2) ≜ ( a1 ⊓1 b1 , a2 ⊓2 b2 ) ;
• ⊥ ≜ ( ⊥1 , ⊥ 2 ) ;
• ⊤ ≜ ( ⊤1 , ⊤ 2 ) .

===== slide 36 =====
Point-wise lifting

Given the (complete) lattice ( L1 , ⊑1 , ⊔1 , ⊓1 , ⊥1 , ⊤1 ) and a set X
We derive its pointwise lifting lattice ( L, ⊑, ⊔, ⊓, ⊥, ⊤ ) by taking:
x, y ∈ X, l ∈ L ∖{ ⊥ } we can have {x ↦ ⊥ , y ↦ l}
• L ≜ X → L1 ;
• f ⊑ g iff ∀x ∈ X : f(x) ⊑1 g(x) ;
1

L1

• ( f ⊔ g) ≜ λx . f(x) ⊔1 g(x) ;
• ( f ⊓ g) ≜ λx . f(x) ⊓1 g(x) ;
• ⊥ ≜ λx . ⊥1 ;
• ⊤ ≜ λx . ⊤1 .
